1: angelon ischuron katabainonta ek tou ouranou — "a mighty angel coming down from heaven." This angel is distinct from the seven trumpet-angels; his appearance (cloud-wrapped, rainbow crown, sun-face, fire-pillar legs) is theophanic — resembling the divine appearances of Revelation 4 and Ezek 1. iris epi tēs kephalēs — "a rainbow above his head." The rainbow (iris) appears in Ezekiel 1:28 as the sign of the divine glory (the appearance of the likeness of the glory of the LORD was like a rainbow in a cloud on a rainy day). The angel's rainbow-crown connects him to the covenant-faithfulness symbolized by the rainbow since Genesis 9:13.
2: biblaridion ēneōgmenon — "a little scroll lying open." The diminutive biblaridion (little scroll) distinguishes it from the great sealed scroll of Revelation 5 (biblion). This scroll is already open — the contents are accessible. John will eat it in v9-10. The small open scroll stands in contrast to the sealed closed scroll of Revelation 5: a specific, limited prophetic commission rather than the entire cosmic plan.
3: hōsper leōn mykatai — "like a lion roaring" (mykaomai, the bellow of a lion or bull). The lion's roar as a divine-speech metaphor appears in Hosea 11:10 and Joel 3:16 ("YHWH roars from Zion"). The seven thunders speak — the number seven signals completeness; John is about to record their speech when he is commanded to seal it.
4: sphragison ha elalēsan hoi hepta brontai kai mē auta grapsēs — "seal up what the seven thunders said and do not write it." The command to seal recalls Daniel 8:26 ("seal up the vision, for it is for many days") and Daniel 12:4,9 ("close up and seal the words of the scroll until the time of the end"). This is the only content in Revelation that is explicitly withheld from the reader — a deliberate gap in the revelation that resists the apocalyptic reader's desire for complete knowledge. Contrast Revelation 22:10 ("Do not seal up the words of the prophecy of this scroll, because the time is near").
5: ton dexion cheira autou eis ton ouranon — "raised his right hand toward heaven." The oath-gesture: lifting the right hand toward heaven in the swearing of an oath (Deuteronomy 32:40; Daniel 12:7: "the man dressed in linen raised his right hand and his left hand toward heaven and swore by him who lives forever"). The angel's swearing by the living God (v6) is a direct allusion to Daniel 12:7.
6: chronos ouketi estai — "there will be no more delay" (or: "time will be no more"). The Greek is ambiguous: chronos can mean "delay" (as in "no more waiting") or "time" (as in eschatological time ending). Most interpreters favor "delay" given the context of the imminent seventh trumpet and the completion of the mystery. hos ektisen ton ouranon ... gēn ... thalassan — the oath is sworn by the Creator of heaven, earth, and sea (the tripartite creation formula from Gen 1, repeated in the Decalogue: Exodus 20:11).
7: etelesthē to mystērion tou Theou — "the mystery of God will be fulfilled." Mystērion in Paul refers to the previously hidden divine plan now revealed in Christ (Romans 16:25; Ephesians 1:9; Revelation 3:3-9; Colossians 1:26-27). Here, the mystery is the complete plan of salvation-through-judgment that the prophets announced but only partially saw. Euēngelisen (announced the good news) — the mystery was previewed as euangelion (gospel): even the judgment-plan is good news in Revelation's theodicy.
8: hypage labein to biblaridion — "go take the little scroll." John is commanded to take active possession: not to receive passively but to approach and take. The two-part transaction (request and grant, vv8-9) before the eating foreshadows the bitter-sweet experience — the prophet must want and accept the prophetic commission before experiencing its weight.
9: pikranei sou tēn koilian alla en tō stomati sou estai glykу hōs meli — "it will make your stomach bitter, but in your mouth it will be as sweet as honey." Ezekiel 2:8-3:3: Ezekiel ate a scroll and it was sweet as honey — but Ezekiel was then sent to speak hard words to a rebellious people. The bitterness-after-sweetness in John's commissioning adds the experiential weight that Ezekiel's version lacked: the prophetic word is genuinely sweet to receive (it is God's word) and genuinely bitter to carry (its content is judgment).
10: ēn glykу hōs meli ... epikranthē hē koilia mou — "it was sweet as honey... my stomach was made bitter" (aorist passive). The aorist epikranthē (was embittered) marks the bitterness as a specific, completed experience. The mouth-to-stomach progression maps the prophetic experience: the initial reception of God's word is joyful; the internalization and digestion of its meaning (the full weight of the judgments to come) is painful. The prophet does not remain at a distance from his message.
11: dei se palin prophēteusai — "it is necessary for you to prophesy again." Dei (it is necessary/must) is the divine-necessity verb in the NT (used for Christ's passion in Luke 24:7; for Paul's preaching in Acts 9:6; for apocalyptic events in Revelation 1:1; Revelation 4:1). John's prophetic commission is renewed after the scroll-eating: the re-commissioning mid-vision (like Ezekiel's re-commissioning in Ezekiel 3:4-11) sends him forward with an expanded mandate: "peoples, nations, languages, and kings."
